an overexplainer of my favourite proof that there are infinitely many primes, for non-mathematicians

(note: if you're a mathematician and just want to see the proof, scroll to the bottom!)

Part 1: "Wait Prof what does the title of this post even mean?"

Take your favourite positive integer (i.e. a counting number) greater than 1, and try writing it as the product of strictly smaller numbers. (For example, we can write 10=2⋅5, but not 10=10⋅1.) Then write those numbers as products of strictly smaller numbers, and keep going until you can't anymore. If you try this for a few different numbers, you'll notice that you can find some numbers (2, 3, 5, 7, ...) but not others (4, 6, 8, 9, ...) in your product. This is interesting enough that mathematicians have a name for it: The first group of numbers form the prime numbers, while the second group forms the composite numbers. (Another, equivalent definition is that a prime number is any positive integer with exactly two positive factors. This is the definition you probably learned in school, but I personally think this doesn't do a very good job of explaining why these numbers are interesting.)

There are infinitely many composite numbers: A good way to see this is that we can write

4=2⋅2, 8=2⋅2⋅2, 16=2⋅2⋅2⋅2, 32=2⋅2⋅2⋅2⋅2,

and so on infinitely, so these numbers must all be composite. But are there infinitely many prime numbers?

Yeah, there are.

The Ancient Greek mathematician Euclid was the first to show that there were infinitely many primes around 2500 years ago. The key idea behind Euclid's original proof is that if you take the product of a bunch of numbers (that aren't 1) and add 1 to that product, the resulting number can't be divisible by any of the numbers you used to make the product. For example, 2⋅3⋅5+1=31, and 31 isn't divisible by any of 2, 3, and 5: If it were, then at least one of the fractions 31/2, 31/3, and 31/5 would be an integer. Here's why this is relevant: If 2, 3, and 5 were the only prime numbers, we should be able to break down 31 as a product of 2's, 3's, and 5's. But 31 isn't divisible by 2, 3, or 5, so 31 must be a prime number... wait, what? That's impossible, so 2, 3, and 5 can't be the only prime numbers.

Here's a larger example: Suppose that 2, 3, 5, 7, and 11 are the only prime numbers. Since 2⋅3⋅5⋅7⋅11+1 is larger than 11, we should be able to write this as the product of 2's, 3's, 5's, 7's, and 11's. But this number isn't divisible by any of 2, 3, 5, 7, and 11: For example, it's not divisible by 2 since 2⋅3⋅5⋅7⋅11+12=3⋅5⋅7⋅11+12,which isn't an integer. Similarly, dividing this number by 3 yields an integer with a fraction of 1/3 left over, and this pattern continues for 5, 7, and 11. So we can't actually write this number as a product of 2's, 3's, 5's, 7's, and/or 11's - but this contradicts what we found earlier, so there have to be more prime numbers than just 2, 3, 5, 7, and 11. The same idea works if we use any finite list of prime numbers instead of just 2, 3, 5, 7, and 11, so there must be infinitely many primes.

This is a pretty proof, but it's by no means my favourite proof of this fact. We'll get there, but first we need some background, starting with

Part 2: How to split a pizza

Suppose that you and two friends order a square pizza, and that it arrives unsliced. Unfortunately, none of you can agree on where the one-third line of a side actually is, so dividing the pizza into thirds by making two parallel cuts isn't an option. On the other hand, you all definitely agree on how to cut something into quarters. How do you evenly split the pizza, if no one cares about getting more or less crust?

Here's one way: Take the pizza and divide it into quarters. Give one quarter to each person. Then, take the remaining quarter and divide that into quarters. Again, give one quarter-quarter to each person. Take the remaining quarter-quarter and divide that into quarters. Give one quarter-quarter-quarter to each person, and keep going until your pizza gets cold. Basically, divide up the pizza as shown below, and give all the slices coloured blue to one person, all the slices coloured red to another person, and all the slices coloured green to the third person.

So each person gets 1/3 of the pizza this way, and everyone's happy. But I'm getting a little ahead of myself here - let's trace back through what happened at each step. When the pizza's first divided into slices, each person gets 1/4 of the pizza. When the remaining slice is divided into four smaller slices, each person then gets another 1/16 of the pizza. When the remaining tiny bit is divided into four even tinier slices, each person then gets another 1/64 of the pizza. This can keep going forever, which tells us that

14+116+164+1256+⋯=13.

You can use the same idea to divide pizzas among more than three people, too: If you need to split your square pizza among five people and can agree on how to cut a pizza into sixths, just cut the pizza into sixths, have everyone take a slice, cut the leftover into sixths, have everyone take a slice of that, and so on until your pizza gets cold.

This in turn shows that

16+162+163+164+⋯=15.

More generally, for any positive integer n, you can use the same idea to show that

1n+1n2+1n3+1n4+⋯=1n−1,

and adding 1 to both sides of this equation gives us

1+1n+1n2+1n3+1n4+⋯=nn−1.

We'll come back to this equation in a bit, but first, a detour: What happens if you try and multiply two of these infinite monsters? And how do you even multiply two of these things anyway?

Part 3: FOIL 2: Electric Boogaloo

If you took an English-language algebra class in North America growing up, chances are the word "FOIL" pokes something long untouched in your brain. Well, I'm here to tell you that unfortunately, memorizing this was a bit pointless: This is really just an example of the distributive property, which essentially says that to multiply two sums, you just have to multiply each thing in the first sum by each thing in the second sum. Here's a visual demonstration of how we can multiply two numbers using the distributive property:

696⋅69=(600+90+6)⋅(60+9)=600⋅(60+9)+90⋅(60+9)+6⋅(60+9)=600⋅60+600⋅9+90⋅60+90⋅9+6⋅60+6⋅9=36000+5400+5400+810+360+54=48024

So how can we multiply two infinite sums? Well, we can just do this infinitely many times:

11319127⋮111319127⋮121216118154⋮14141121361108⋮18181241721216⋮⋯⋯⋯⋯⋯⋱ (1+12+14+18+⋯)⋅(1+13+19+127+⋯)=1+12+13+14+16+18+19+112+118+⋯

What's going on here? All the fractions in the new sum are of the form 1/(something), but not every fraction of the form 1/(something) is here. Looking more closely at what "somethings" are actually here, it looks like when we try to write each of these numbers (except for 1) as a product of smaller numbers (as we did right at the start of this journey), we'll only end up with 2's and 3's at the end. Also, every number that can be broken down exclusively into 2's and 3's is here: Each "something" from the fractions in the first sum could only be broken down into 2's, and we had every such fraction there, since the sum was infinite. Similarly, each "something" from the fractions in the second sum could only be broken down into 3's, and the infiniteness of the sum guarantees we have every such fraction there. Since we multiply the two sums by multiplying each thing in the first sum by each thing in the second sum, we're guaranteed to get every number that can be broken down into 2's and 3's in the final sum.

If we try this a few times, we'd notice that this weird behaviour only happens if we multiply two sums beginning with 1+1/(a prime number)+.... And that gets us thinking: What happens if we multiply all of these sums? Well, the resulting sum will have the number 1, and then every fraction of the form 1/(something), where "something" can be written as a product of 2's, 3's, 5's, 7's, and so on. But these are the prime numbers, so the "something" can in fact be any positive integer! This tells us that

(1+12+14+⋯)⋅(1+13+19+⋯)⋅(1+15+125+⋯)⋯=1+12+13+14+15+16+17+⋯.

Looking at this, one might wonder: How much would I need to spend to buy this many pizzas?

Part 4: Putting it all together

OK, remember how we thought about this equation earlier?

1+1n+1n2+1n3+1n4+⋯=nn−1

If we combine this with the weird sum we got when we multiplied all the equations starting with 1+1/(a prime number)+..., we find that

(1+12+⋯)(1+13+⋯)(1+15+⋯)⋯=21⋅32⋅54⋅76⋅1110⋯=1+12+13+14+15+⋯.

If there are only finitely many primes, then there would be finitely many terms in the product 21⋅32⋅54⋅76⋅1110⋯.Each term of the product is finite, so the whole product would be finite.

But this product is equal to the sum 1+12+13+14+15+⋯.And as it turns out, this sum is infinite: Suppose we had infinitely many pizza slices measuring 1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4 of a pizza and so on, and that we wanted to give all these slices away to a bunch of people, making sure that each person gets at least half a pizza. How many people would we need?

Well, we could give 1 pizza to the first person, and 1/2 a pizza to the next person - that's easy enough. We could then give the next person the 1/3 and 1/4 slices: A third is more than a quarter and two quarters make a half, so this is more than half a pizza. Then, the next person can get the four slices measuring 1/5, 1/6, 1/7, and 1/8 of a pizza: Each of these is at least an eighth of a pizza and four eighths make a half, so this is more than half a pizza. The next 8 slices are each at least 1/16 of a pizza and eight sixteenths make a half, so we could give those to the next person. For each successive person, we could give them twice as many slices that are just over half as large - and because we started with infinitely many slices, we'll never run out of pizza, no matter how many folks we want to feed!

Working backwards now, we see that since this sum is infinite, the product 21⋅32⋅54⋅76⋅1110⋯.must be too. But this can't happen if there are only finitely many primes, so there must be infinitely many primes!

Epilogue: "OK, but how much of an overexplainer was this really?"

As it turns out, all of the above can be written in one sentence if you speak math: Note that

∏p primepp−1=∏p prime(∑i=0∞1pi)=∑i=1∞1i

is infinite, which can't happen if there are only finitely many primes.
